module_map.responsibility: HTTP request lifecycle: middleware chain construction (sync+async), request dispatch, exception-to-response conversion, view wrapping with atomic transactions
Request and response abstractions: HttpRequest (with META, FILES, POST parsing), HttpResponse hierarchy, cookies, streaming, multipart parsing
URL pattern matching and reverse resolution: compile regex/path routes, resolve path to callback+kwargs, reverse named URLs
ORM: model definition, queryset API (lazy SQL generation), backend connections, transactions, migrations
Pluggable request/response processors: CSRF protection, caching, security headers, gzip, locale, CSP, common redirects
View base classes (View, TemplateView, generic CRUD), error pages, CSRF error view, static file serving
Template engine: parsing, context, tag/filter library, multiple backends, lazy TemplateResponse
Global settings via lazy LazySettings proxy, default values in global_settings, app-registry bootstrap
Application registry: discover installed apps, populate model registry, resolve app labels and models
Signal/event bus: weakref-based signal registration and emission used for request lifecycle hooks
Shared utilities: encoding, functional helpers (lazy, cached_property), deprecation, datastructures, async helpers, logging, decorators
Form and field validation, widget rendering, model forms, formsets

pipeline_trace.description: get_asgi_application() or get_wsgi_application() instantiates ASGIHandler/WSGIHandler, which inherits from BaseHandler. load_middleware() builds the _middleware_chain callable by wrapping handlers in order from settings.MIDDLEWARE.

get_response() / get_response_async() is called with an HttpRequest. The _middleware_chain invokes each middleware's process_request hook in sequence. BaseHandler.adapt_method_mode wraps sync middleware for async contexts and vice-versa using asgiref sync_to_async / async_to_sync.

BaseHandler.resolve_request() calls get_resolver(urlconf).resolve(request.path_info). URLResolver traverses URLPattern/URLResolver tree, matching RoutePattern or RegexPattern. Returns a ResolverMatch containing the view callable, args, kwargs, url_name, and namespaces. Result is stored as request.resolver_match.

_get_response() iterates middleware list calling process_view(request, callback, callback_args, callback_kwargs) on each. Any middleware returning a non-None response short-circuits the view. make_view_atomic() optionally wraps the view in a database transaction.

wrapped_callback(request, *callback_args, **callback_kwargs) is called. Class-based views call View.as_view() which routes to get()/post() etc. Views may read request.GET/POST/FILES (QueryDict). Function views return an HttpResponse directly.

Views invoke Model.objects.filter()/get()/etc. returning a QuerySet. SQL is lazily compiled via QuerySet._iterator() / SQLCompiler. Results are returned as model instances. Connection from django.db.connections[alias] manages the database cursor.

TemplateResponse.render() calls Engine.get_template().render(context, request). Context merges template variables with context processors. Result is written to HttpResponse.content (bytes). FileResponse or StreamingHttpResponse may be used for large payloads.

Middleware process_response hooks run in reverse order (innermost first). CommonMiddleware may redirect; CsrfViewMiddleware sets CSRF cookie; SecurityMiddleware adds headers. log_response() logs 4xx/5xx. Final HttpResponse is returned to the WSGI/ASGI server.

cross_module_hubs.reason: Virtually every other module imports from django.utils. MiddlewareMixin (utils.deprecation) is the base for all middleware; cached_property and LazyObject (utils.functional) are used in models, settings, and URLs; encoding, html, and datastructures utilities are consumed across forms, templates, ORM, and HTTP layers.

The LazySettings singleton is imported in nearly every module that needs runtime configuration (MIDDLEWARE, DATABASES, TEMPLATES, INSTALLED_APPS, DEBUG, etc.). It is the primary global dependency injection point for the entire framework.

Shared exception types (ImproperlyConfigured, ValidationError, PermissionDenied, SuspiciousOperation, ObjectDoesNotExist, MiddlewareNotUsed) are raised and caught across db, views, forms, middleware, and urls, making this a structural hub for error propagation.

change_proposal.feature_summary: Add AsyncStructuredLoggingMiddleware: an async-compatible middleware that times each request, captures view name from ResolverMatch, counts ORM queries via the connection query log, and emits a single structured log entry (JSON-serializable dict) at response time using Python's logging module.
new_types: AsyncStructuredLoggingMiddleware(MiddlewareMixin): async-capable middleware class with __init__, __call__ (async), and process_response; stores request start time in request._slm_start; emits structured log on response
RequestLogRecord(TypedDict): typed container for log fields: method, path, view_name, status_code, duration_ms, query_count, user_agent
change_proposal.pattern_to_follow: Follow django/middleware/common.py and django/middleware/security.py: subclass MiddlewareMixin from django.utils.deprecation, implement process_request (set start timestamp on request) and process_response (compute duration, collect query count, call logger.info with structured dict). Use async_capable = True and sync_capable = True flags as done in CsrfViewMiddleware. Use django.utils.log logger conventions (getLogger(__name__)).
change_proposal.integration_point: Add to settings.MIDDLEWARE after SecurityMiddleware but before CommonMiddleware so it captures the full request/response cycle. ORM query count is read from django.db.connection.queries (requires DEBUG=True) or a custom signal counter via django.db.backends.signals.connection_created for production use. View name is extracted from request.resolver_match.view_name set by BaseHandler.resolve_request().
risks: Query counting via connection.queries only works when DEBUG=True; production use requires hooking into db signals or a custom DatabaseWrapper, adding complexity.

Storing timing state on the request object (request._slm_start) creates implicit coupling; concurrent async requests to the same handler instance are safe because each request is a distinct HttpRequest object, but this must be documented.

MiddlewareMixin's adapt_method_mode wrapping may add latency overhead if the middleware is not declared with both async_capable and sync_capable, causing unnecessary thread/coroutine switching on every request.
Structured log output format is not standardized in Django; consumers must agree on field names, and any schema change is a breaking change for log aggregation pipelines.
Adding a middleware to the default MIDDLEWARE list (if done) would affect all projects on upgrade; it should be opt-in only.
